questions.question: What is the correct way to activate a Spring profile in a production environment?
options: Add @Profile("prod") to the main application class
Set the environment variable SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod
Create a file named active-profile.txt with the word 'prod'
Set spring.profiles.default=prod in application-prod.properties

questions.question: A @Service class is annotated with @Profile("dev"). What happens when the application starts with spring.profiles.active=prod?
options: The service is created but its methods are no-ops
Spring throws NoSuchBeanDefinitionException at startup
The bean is not created — Spring ignores all @Profile beans that don't match the active profile
The service is created but wrapped in a proxy that disables its methods

questions.question: Which profile selector creates a bean for ALL environments EXCEPT production?
options: @Profile("dev|staging|test")
@Profile({"dev", "staging", "test"})
@Profile("!prod")
@Profile(exclude = "prod")

questions.question: In application-dev.properties, which ddl-auto value is appropriate for development but dangerous in production?
options: validate — checks schema matches entities
none — no schema management
create-drop — creates schema on start, drops on stop
update — modifies existing schema to match entities

questions.question: What does @ActiveProfiles("test") on a @SpringBootTest class do?
options: It disables security for all test methods
It activates the 'test' profile, loading application-test.properties and profile-specific beans
It automatically creates mock beans for all @Autowired dependencies
It runs each test in an isolated Spring context
